Empires & Puzzles Tenebrae Review: The Dark Construct's Fatal Flaw

Tenebrae arrives with stunning 'Dark Souls' inspired art and a mechanic that sounds great on paper but confusing in practice. Is this Construct sniper a tactical genius or just a beautiful liability? We break down the math behind the Soul Bound skill.
Tenebrae: The Soul-Bound Sniper Who Hurts Herself to Hurt You
Tenebrae is a 5-star Dark (Purple) hero from the
family, originally released in October 2023 via the Covenant Summon. She enters the arena with one of the most visually striking designs in the game and a mechanic—
—that promises to turn your opponent's aggression against them.

However, Tenebrae is a complex puzzle. She is a Fast sniper who wants to take damage to be effective, a paradox that has left many players scratching their heads. In an era of simple "delete button" heroes, Tenebrae requires setup, timing, and a bit of masochism to work.
Let's just say it: Tenebrae wins the fashion show. Her design is heavily reminiscent of the

, featuring a lithe, armored, spectral aesthetic with glowing purple accents. It is haunting, elegant, and arguably one of the best pieces of character art Small Giant has produced. If looks could kill, she’d be top-tier. Unfortunately, in Empires & Puzzles, you need more than a cool helmet to survive.
was headline news—she was one of the first to break the 1k barrier. Today, that stat line is dangerously close to becoming average.
Comparing her to the modern meta (late 2025/early 2026):
She is significantly squishier than modern heroes. With under 1600 base HP, she melts under the pressure of modern 500%+ snipers, which is ironic because her primary mechanic
This skill is a mixed bag of "Okay" damage and "Weird" utility.

To match the damage output of a top-tier sniper, Tenebrae needs to "reflect" about 2500 raw damage worth of hits over 3 turns without dying. Given her low defense, taking that much damage is risky.
Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities
family offers one of the more interesting passive systems in the game, which is often the real reason to field Tenebrae.
The family bonus gives a chance to cast a "weak" version of the Construct Core effect when hit. It's unreliable but nice when it triggers.
is the star here. Every time she fires:
per turn (stacking naturally as they gain mana).
Deals extra damage to minion owners.
This passive effectively turns her single-target skill into a soft AoE that disrupts the entire enemy team's timing. It is the glue holding her kit together.
. While it helps her initial snipe and tile damage, a defensive Aether Power (like Damage Reduction or Revive) would have been infinitely better to help her survive long enough to utilize Soul Bound.
Upon release, Tenebrae was met with confusion. Players immediately compared her to
and found her wanting. She was considered "balanced" in an era of overpowered heroes, which usually means "underpowered" in practice.

The game has moved away from simple snipers. Heroes now do three or four things at once (Damage + Heal + Cleanse + Buff). Tenebrae does Damage + Weird Debuff. Her stats have fallen behind significantly, making her a liability in defense.

She is essentially a fun toy for offense when you want to experiment, but she is too risky for competitive Wars or Raids. Her poison is good, but you can get similar effects from better Construct heroes like

Her Core activates on cast, providing passive damage and mana control, and she's Fast.
The poison adds up, and Soul Bound prevents enemies from "healing" via damage share (since they take damage instead).
Can be used to surgically remove a specific threat like a reposter or a passive tank, provided you can keep her alive.
Tenebrae works best when you force the opponent into a "damned if you do, damned if you don't" situation.
Use Tenebrae's core to slow them down, lock their mana with a silencer, and let the poison whittle them away.

If you pulled Tenebrae early, she is a solid sniper. The Corrosive Poison is powerful in map stages and lower-tier raids. Level her, but be aware that she is squishy. Do not rely on the Soul Bound mechanic to win games; treat it as a bonus.

She is a bench warmer. Unless you are playing in a restricted tournament or just want to have fun with the mechanic, there are likely 10 other Dark heroes in your roster who do the job better. Do not waste Alpha Aethers here unless you are a "Construct Family Completionist."
The community has had a love-hate relationship with Tenebrae, mostly leaning towards disappointment.
Beta testers recall she originally had a
effect on the target, which was removed before release. Many believe this nerf killed her viability:
"I think they destroyed her by taking away silence."
One user perfectly summed it up:
"It feels like a weak version of Quintin. Relying on the enemy is random at best."
She’s often mockingly referred to as having a "suicide skill"—you have to let her get beat up to deal damage.
"The collector in me wants her for the design... one of the best arts in the game."

Tenebrae is a tragedy of game design. She has the looks of a goddess and the kit of a confused sidekick. The Soul Bound mechanic is a fascinating concept that was played too safely, resulting in a hero who is scared to take a hit despite needing to.
If she receives a buff (perhaps damage reduction or healing on damage received), she could rise from the ashes. Until then, she remains a beautiful statue in the museum of Empires & Puzzles.
Bench (unless you really need the Poison).
A detailed review of Tenebrae, the 5-star Dark hero from the Construct family. We analyze her unique Soul Bound mechanic, Corrosive Core, and whether she's worth your resources in the current meta.
